result:
I keep thinking we have not yet built a tool worthy of the word “mirror.”  
Our phones, our watches, our webcams: they are carnival mirrors—stretching, squeezing, tinting—because they reflect us only through the profit-seeking filter of engagement. Tell me: when was the last time you opened an app and felt more yourself after you closed it? When did the timeline end not in dizziness but in clarity?

There is a deeper mirror waiting to be invented, one built inside silence instead of on top of metrics. Imagine a low screen—no brighter than candlelight—sitting on your desk next to the coffee ring. It has one button. You press it, the screen stays black for three full breaths, then a single short question fades in:  
What did you come to tell yourself?

You answer aloud or in writing or only in the minute nod of your own pupils; the device does not care. It does not store, rank, or share. The words—yours—fall into a tiny mirrored chamber inside the shell. Slowly, the letters emerge again, not as text but as light distorted through slightly curved glass so that you must see them backward, like reading your own face in a restroom mirror after crying. The question folds into its own answer. The device then rests until tomorrow, like a well-walked dog.

We do not need smarter machines; we need machines that refuse to be smarter than a forest floor. Technology suffocates me with second chances: retweet, relive, reshare. But forest floors offer only one chance—this instant’s rot becoming next spring’s moss—and by that unrepeatability they become timeless. I want a piece of hardware whose hottest state is lukewarm, whose brightest glow is moonlight, whose job is to hold a place empty long enough for a human moment to fill it.

While we wait, I practice by staring at actual puddles after rain. They are terrible at resolution, perfect at honesty. One blink and the self reflected there is gone.

message.content:
